subtopics.title: Univariate Analysis
subtopics.explanation: Analyzing one variable at a time using summary statistics (mean, median) and visualizations (histograms, boxplots). It helps understand distribution, central tendency, and spread.
subtopics.example: Using a histogram to visualize the distribution of customer ages reveals if the data is skewed or has multiple peaks.
subtopics.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
sns.histplot(df['age'], bins=20, kde=True)
plt.title('Distribution of Age')
plt.show()

subtopics.title: Bivariate Analysis
subtopics.explanation: Exploring relationships between two variables using scatter plots, correlation coefficients, and cross-tabulations. It reveals patterns, correlations, and potential causal relationships.
subtopics.example: A scatter plot of hours studied vs. exam scores can show if more study time is associated with higher scores.
subtopics.code:
sns.scatterplot(x='hours_studied', y='exam_score', data=df)
plt.title('Study Hours vs Exam Score')
plt.show()

subtopics.title: Multivariate Analysis
subtopics.explanation: Analyzing three or more variables simultaneously using techniques like pair plots, heatmaps, and PCA. It uncovers complex interactions and patterns not visible in univariate or bivariate analysis.
subtopics.example: A correlation heatmap shows relationships among multiple features, helping identify multicollinearity or key predictors.
subtopics.code:
sns.heatmap(df.corr(), annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title('Correlation Matrix')
plt.show()

subtopics.title: Data Visualization
subtopics.explanation: Using charts and graphs to communicate insights effectively. Good visualizations make complex data understandable and help identify patterns, outliers, and trends.
subtopics.example: A line chart showing sales over time reveals seasonality and growth trends at a glance.
subtopics.code:
plt.plot(df['date'], df['sales'])
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Sales')
plt.title('Sales Trend')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
